coredata.dc:title: Modulation of red-light emission from carbon quantum dots in acid-based environment and the detection of chromium (Ⅲ) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Ju
Zhang, Jin
Zhang, Weizuo
Xiao, Yiming
Shi, Yanli
Kong, Fanquan
Xu, Wen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the carbon quantum dots, which can emit sharp red light (R-CQDs) under optical excitation, were synthesized via simple heat treatment of wine lees. The features of the photoluminescence (PL) emission from R-CQDs in solutions with different pH values and with metal ions were examined. The intensity of the PL emission from R-CQDs depends sensitively on the pH values of the solutions with the R-CQDs. Interestingly, the pH-response of the PL intensity is reversible and can be enhanced linearly in the pH value range from 4 to 12, indicating that the R-CQDs can be applied for the pH sensor. Furthermore, the quench of the PL emission from R-CQDs can be observed when adding chromium (III) ions into the solution with R-CQDs. This effect can be utilized for selective and quick detection of chromium (III) ions in solutions simply via the standard PL measurement. The interesting and important findings from this work can be applied to explore more practical applications of the CQDs prepared cheaply and easily.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emitting carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescence
pH sensor
Chromium (III) ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of pH values and Cr (Ⅲ) ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of R-CQDs in (a) and their lattice fringes in (b). The diameter distribution of the R-CQDs is shown in (c).

XRD spectrum of R-CQDs in (a) and XRD standard card in (b). The full-scan XPS spectrum in (c), the high-resolution XPS spectrum for N1s in (d), the FT-IR spectrum of wine lees (blue curve) and R-CQDs (red curve) in (e), and the UV-Vis absorption (black curve) spectrum, the PL excitation spectra (PLE, blue curve) and the PL emission spectra (red curve) in (f) in which the inset shows the photographs of the R-CQDs under ambient light (left bottle) and UV light irradiation (right bottle) for R-CQDs dispersed in ethanol solution. The schematic illustration of the electronic energy levels and the electronic transition channels of the R-CQDs are shown in (g).

PL spectra for CQDs dispersed in pure ethanol solution (a), in a mixed ethanol and NaOH solution (b), and in a mixed ethanol and HCl solution (c). The corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of R-CQDs in these solutions are shown respectively in (d), (e), and (f). The insets in (a, b, and c) show the chromaticity diagrams of R-CQDs.
Intensity of TRPL as a function of delay time. The dots are experimental results and the curve is given by Eq. (1) with fitting parameters as indicated.

(a) PL spectra of the R-CQDs with different pH values increased from 4 to 12, where the inset shows the relationship between PL amplitudes and pH values. (b) The PL spectra of the R-CQDs with the pH values decreased from 12 to 4, in which the inset shows the number of reversible fluorescence of R-CQDs. The excitation wavelength for (a) and (b) is fixed at 420 nm. The structure diagrams of pure R-CQDs in (c) and of R-CQDs in sodium hydroxide in (d).
TGA (blue curve) and the DGT (red curve) of R-CQDs.

(a) The relative PL intensities (or peak amplitudes) of R-CQDs in ethanol solutions with various metal ions at 1 mM concentration, measured at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm in which the blue histogram shows the relative PL intensity of R-CQDs solutions with Cr3+. (b) The absorption spectra of the R-CQDs in pure ethanol solution (red curve) and in ethanol solution with Cr3+ ions (blue curve). (c, d) The PL emission spectra for R-CQDs with different concentrations N of Cr3+ ions, where N=[0.0, 0.6] μM and N=[1, 80] μM show different linear dependencies as shown in the inserts.

The coordination diagram of Cr3+ with hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino functional groups on the surface of R-CQDs in (a), (b) and (c) respectively. The schematic diagram of R-CQDs quenching process as shown in (d).
Detection limit and pH-sensitivity of different CQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: CNPs = Carbon nano-particles; CDs = Carbon dots; N/A = Not available.

head.ce:title: Modulation of red-light emission from carbon quantum dots in acid-based environment and the detection of chromium (Ⅲ) ions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Physics and Astronomy and Key Laboratory of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province, Yunnan University, Kunming, 650091, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Astronomy and Key Laboratory of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the carbon quantum dots, which can emit sharp red light (R-CQDs) under optical excitation, were synthesized via simple heat treatment of wine lees. The features of the photoluminescence (PL) emission from R-CQDs in solutions with different pH values and with metal ions were examined. The intensity of the PL emission from R-CQDs depends sensitively on the pH values of the solutions with the R-CQDs. Interestingly, the pH-response of the PL intensity is reversible and can be enhanced linearly in the pH value range from 4 to 12, indicating that the R-CQDs can be applied for the pH sensor. Furthermore, the quench of the PL emission from R-CQDs can be observed when adding chromium (III) ions into the solution with R-CQDs. This effect can be utilized for selective and quick detection of chromium (III) ions in solutions simply via the standard PL measurement. The interesting and important findings from this work can be applied to explore more practical applications of the CQDs prepared cheaply and easily.

ce:section.ce:para: It is known that semiconductor-based quantum dots are usually extracted initially from a mixture of lead, cadmium, and silicon, while the issue of toxicity has to be considered seriously for their fabrication and practical applications. In recent years, researchers have been attempting to extract the quantum dots from benign compounds. In particular, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials were first obtained in 2004 [1]. It has been found that the brightly luminescent CQDs are with similar physical and chemical properties of semiconductor-based quantum dots [2]. On the other hand, the CQDs with relatively low toxicity, coupled with the flexibility for surface modification [3], make them the most suitable alternatives to metal nanoclusters and traditional dye molecules for optoelectronic applications. In recent years, the investigation into the unique fluorescent properties of the CQDs has become a fast-growing field of research in condensed matter physics, chemistry, and material science.

In the early years, when the CQDs were discovered, the quantum yield (QY) of the photoluminescence (PL) from the CQDs is extremely low, even less than 5 %. In 2006 [4] Sun and co-workers modified the CQDs with diamine-terminated organic polymers and found that the passivated CQDs with functional groups on the surface of the CQDs can exhibit obvious light luminescence under the optical pumping. Thus, the QY of the CQDs can reach up to 10 % [4], where the active centers of the fluorescent emission from the CQDs come from chemical functional groups attached to the surface and/or edges of the cores of the CQDs. These functional groups can provide more channels for radiative electronic transitions accompanied by the emission of photons. Since then, nitrogen (N) was doped into the CQDs during the preparation process, which can result in higher QY [2,4–6]. In 2012, Liu et al. synthetized the CQDs from grass by hydrothermal treatment [7], which opened up a window for the fabrication of the CQDs from natural substances. More remarkably, Liang et al. prepared the CQDs with tunable emission ranging from blue to near-infrared [8]. They found that the mechanism of the tunable PL is attributed mainly to the surface state and N element content in the CQDs [8]. With increasing the N element content in the CQDs, the spectra of the PL emission from the CQDs can be shifted from blue to near-infrared (NIR) bandwidths. This work is worthy for reference in the field of the preparation of the red light-emitting CQDs.

Over the past few years, the publications related to the investigation of the CQDs have shown an exponential growth [5] of the achievement in the field, which indicate that remarkable progress has been made in the synthesis methods, the luminescent mechanism, and the application of the CQDs. To date, the CQDs have been taken as optoelectronic materials for widespread applications in the areas such as fluorescent probes [6], optoelectronic devices [9,10], photocatalytic [11], to mention but a few. However, the further applications of the CQDs from the current fabrication technology still face some to be solved problems. (i) The PL emission spectrum crossing the entire visible bandwidth is a key issue for the CQDs to meet the requirements for the practical application in, e.g., display and LED [12]. Nevertheless, most of the optical absorption peaks in the CQDs are located in the range from 190 to 350 nm (i.e., the UV region) and most of the PL emission wavelength from the CQDs are in the scope of 410–550 nm (blue and green light regions), with only a few exceptions [13]. (ii) The element composition and the structure of the CQDs contribute to the diversity of their physical properties [2,5,14]. In many reported works, the CQDs are synthesized by the mixtures of the carbon sources with multiple components. This can result in which the full width at half maximum of the PL emission spectrum from the CQDs is rather broad and even the spectral bands cannot be observed significantly in the absorption spectrum. Furthermore, the corresponding QY has not yet reached the requirements for the application as practical devices. The technique for complete isolation of the CQDs is desperately desired. Thus, it is imperative to realize the red-emitting CQDs with outstanding optical characteristics similar to semiconductor-based quantum dots (such as CdTe and CdSe) with saturated narrow bandwidth emission and sharp and pure color.

In this work, a highly efficient, low cost and environment-friendly synthetic method is developed in fabricating the red-emitting CQDs (R-CQDs) from wine lees via simple heat treatment. Wine lees is an inevitable environmental waste in the process of wine production. They are composed mainly of organic matters such as proteins, fibers, lipids, sugars, and organic acids [15]. Additionally, the wine lees also contain long-chain carboxylic acids [16], which are not easily decomposed during the dehydration and carbonization processes at high temperature. Thus, they can be taken as a passivation agent to increase the stability of the CQDs by preventing aggregation. Therefore, the wine lees are an excellent carbon source for synthesizing the CQDs. It is noteworthy that the R-CQDs can exhibit obvious pH-sensitive merits in the pH value range from 4 to 12. This feature makes them be the materials for potential applications in the area of optical pH sensors. By utilizing the wine lees to fabricate the R-CQDs, a reliable, affordable, and sustainable source can be achieved for making valuable materials such as the CQDs. Furthermore, these R-CQDs made from wine lees can be applied for rapid detection of chromium (III) ions (or Cr3+) in solutions through the standard PL measurement. A mechanism for the quenching of the PL emission from R-CQDs induced by chromium (III) ions is proposed in this paper.

In this study, the R-CQDs were synthesized by the heat treatment of the wine lees which is taken as the carbon source. (i) 100 mL of wine lees was mixed with 200 mL deionized water evenly. The mixture was put into an open beaker, then heated for 0.5–2 h on a heating platform with a temperature of 300 °C to obtain the reactants; (ii) 100 mL ethanol solution (C2H5OH) was added into the beaker after the reactants in the beaker were cooled down; (iii) The reactants were magnetically stirred and further centrifuged. Thus, the supernatant which contains the CQDs can be obtained; And (iv) the supernatant was further post-treated by column chromatography or dialysis tube to achieve size control and uniformity of the CQDs. In such a way, the CQDs can be acquired within the ethanol solution. Finally, the concentration of the CQDs was increased with a rotary evaporator.

In this work, the morphology and microstructures of the R-CQDs realized from wine lees were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TEM 2100, Japan) operated at 300 kV. The crystalline phase of the R-CQDs was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to characterize the samples, by using PHI5000 Versa Probe II photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα at 1486.6 eV. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum was measured by using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Specord200). The Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured on a Nicoletis10 FTIR spectrometer using KBr pressed disks. The measurement of time-resolved PL (TRPL) from R-CQDs was undertaken by FLS 920 Fluorophotometer and the cutoff condition for attenuation is 5000 photons. The thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) and the differential thermo-gravimetric (DGT) were measured by using a Mettler TGA (1600 L F, Swiss) in the temperature range from 50 °C to 500 °C. The photo-induced light emission was examined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (IHR320, HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA) for different excitation wavelengths ranging from 370 nm to 420 nm at room temperature. The fluorescent QY of the CQDs was evaluated on the base of the PL data.

The pH values were adjusted by altering the sodium hydroxide (NaOH powder at analytical pure) and the hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 %, analytical pure) in the solution. For example, when the concentration of NaOH is 0.1 mg/mL (C NaOH =0.1 mg/mL or 0.0025 mol/L), the pH of NaOH solution is about 11.17. When the concentration of HCl is 0.0036 mg/mL (C HCl =0.0036 mg/mL or 0.0001 mol/L), the pH of HCl solution is about 4.22. Other solutions with different pH values were titrated with 1 mol/L NaOH and 1 mol/L HCl according to the reaction equation: H+ + OH− → H2O. At the same time for titration, the pH value is monitored by a pH electronic tester. The concentration of ethanol is 99.9 % analytical pure and it is not electrolyte so that the pH value of ethanol solution is equal to 7. In this work, 5 mL R-CQDs solution was added into 6 mL acidic or alkaline solution (the pH from 2 to 12) to make serial solutions.

Different metal ions including Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag2+, Cr3+, and Mg2+ were added into R-CQDs solution to examine the effect of the metal ions on the PL. Among them, the standard Cr3+ ion solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL was purchased (Nonferrous Metals Grinm, China). The molar mass of Cr atom is 52 g/mol, so the molar concentration of Cr3+ is about 0.02 mol/L (or 0.02 M). It was diluted with deionized water to the required concentrations. In this work, 1 mL Cr3+ ion solution with different Cr3+ concentrations from 0.1–80 μM was added into 3 mL R-CQDs solution to make serial solutions.

The surface morphology of the R-CQDs, measured by HRTEM, is shown in Fig. 1 (a). These R-CQDs are uniform in size and with good dispersion. The lattice fringes are unambiguous and the corresponding lattice spacing is about 0.212 nm as shown in Fig. 1(b). The particle size distribution of these CQDs is 3–9 nm and the average particle size is 6.51 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(c) and obtained from statistical analysis for more than seven hundred CQDs particles by using the Image J software.

The standard X-ray diffraction (XRD) is examined by the crystalline structure of the R-CQDs. In Fig. 2 (a), the XRD pattern of the R-CQDs shows a single broad peak at 27.1°, corresponding to an interlayer spacing d = 3.29 Å and the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with the turbostratic disorder [17]. The corresponding XRD standard card including pattern and card number (00-046-0943) is shown in Fig. 2(b). The TEM and the XRD results indicate that the prepared R-CQDs consist of carbon cores with a carbon lattice structure. In this study, the XPS is applied for the analysis of the elemental and surface functional groups attached to the R-CQDs. In Fig. 2(c), the full-scan XPS spectrum for R-CQDs shows that the atomic percentages of C1s, O1s and N1s are 54.48 %, 43.64 %, and 1.88 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s shown in Fig. 2(d) exhibits three typical peaks corresponding to pyrrolic nitrogen (NCN), graphite nitrogen (N-C3, 399.8 eV), and the amide group (C-NH2, 401.9 eV), respectively [6]. These results indicate that the R-CQDs are doped with N atoms as impurities. The presence of the chemical bands attached to the R-CQDs (red curve) and in the pure wine lees (blue curve) is observed by the FT-IR spectrum as shown in Fig. 2(e). The absorption band around 3400 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching of OH. The characteristic absorption bands of CO, CC/CC and CO correspond to the peaks at around 1650, 1430, and 1060 cm−1, respectively [18], and the absorption bands at 2160 and 1340 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of NC3 and CN bonds, respectively [18,19]. Also, the stretching vibrations of NH or CH can also be observed at 2990, 837, and 707 cm−1 [20,21]. As shown in Fig. 2(e), compared to the chemical vibration bands of pure wine lees, the R-CQDs have more chemical bonds such as CC/CC, CO, CN, and CH/NH which are introduced in the preparation process due to the carbonization of wine lees under the high temperature.

The UV-Vis absorption and the PL spectra of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 2(f). Similar to those reported in the literature [6,21], the CQDs show significant optical absorption in the UV region 236–350 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of CC/CC bands with a tail extending into the visible region. The difference here is that the R-CQDs also show a shoulder peak in the 350–450 nm range, which can be attributed to the n-π* transition of CO and CN bands [8,12]. Compared to the CQDs which emit short-wavelength light, the CQDs which can emit long-wavelength radiation tend to have longer absorption wavelengths. This is a common phenomenon for long-wavelength emission CQDs, as can be seen, e.g., in Refs. [3,14,22]. From the PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the CQDs, the optimal excitation wavelength can be chosen at 420 nm. These results indicate that the surface states induced by chemical bonds or functional groups attached to the carbon cores of the CQDs play a leading role in determining the features of PL emission from R-CQDs [2]. In the insets of Fig. 2(f), the R-CQDs look yellow under the day-light (left bottle) but they look sharply red (right bottle) in the ethanol solution under the UV light irradiation. The schematic diagram of the electronic energy levels and the electronic transition channels of the R-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2(g). The following points are noted. (i) The R-CQDs show significant optical absorption at 287 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of CC/CC bands. Using the equation [23]: (αhv) n =A(hv-E g), where α is absorption coefficient, hv is the energy of the photon, E g is optical band gap, A is a transition probability constant, and n is a simple fraction which relates to the optical absorption process. The energy gap E g [24] of the R-CQDs for π-π* transition can be evaluated. It is found that the intrinsic energy spacing in the cores of the R-CQDs is about 4.32 eV or 287 nm. The PL emission from this channel cannot be observed due to the limitation of the excitation wavelength in the light source. (ii) The PL emission from R-CQDs comes mainly from the radiative surface states induced by chemical bonds or functional groups attached to the carbon cores of the CQDs. (iii) The photons are absorbed by electrons in the CQDs via electronic transitions from lower occupied to higher empty states under optical pumping with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm (2.95 eV). (iv) The photo-excited electrons can be relaxed into the surface states via non-radiative electronic transitions. And (v) the PL emission can be achieved via electronic transitions from the surface states to the lower carbon-based electronic states accompanied by the emission of photons. Therefore, the peak of the PL emission can be observed at about 676 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of pH values and Cr (Ⅲ) ions
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 (a) shows the PL spectra for R-CQDs in pure ethanol solution at different excitation wavelengths. The positions of the PL peaks depend rather weakly on the excitation wavelength in the range from 370 nm to 420 nm, which is typical behavior for R-CQDs with surface passivation [25]. From the chromaticity diagram of the R-CQDs in the illustration in Fig. 3(a) that the fluorescence emission from R-CQDs is mainly in the red color region. This indicates that a narrow bandwidth and pure red-light emission from R-CQDs are achieved, where a relatively small FWHM about 25 nm can be found. Fig. 4 shows the intensity of the time-resolved PL (TRPL) from R-CQDs in pure ethanol, measured by using a multi-dimensional time-correlated single photon count (TCSPC) to evaluate the fluorescence decay behavior [26]. The fluorescence decay can be fitted by a double-exponential decay model through:
(1)
R
t
=
α 0
+
α 1
exp
− t /
τ 1
+
α 2
exp
− t /
τ 2
where α 0 is the background PL intensity and α 1 and α 2 are the fractional contributions to PL emission from two transition channels with corresponding decay time or lifetime τ 1 and τ 2. Through fitting, the channel with τ 1 ∼2.98 ns (Channel-1) contributes 27.2 % of the PL emission, while the channel with τ2 ∼7.80 ns (Channel-2) contributes 72.1 %. Specifically, Channel-1 relates to the transition between the intrinsic C-states in R-CQDs, while Channel-2 corresponds to the transition with the surface states in R-CQDs [27] via non-radiation and radiative electronic relaxations. Therefore, Channel-2 with the surface states of R-CQDs is the major channel for PL emission from R-CQDs. Such a result indicates that the radiative electronic transition can be acquired in nanoseconds in CQDs, which makes the as-prepared R-CQDs as materials for fluorescent sensors in potential applications.

It is known that in contrast to traditional stage energy-controlled sensors [28], the fluorescent nano-sensors with prominent features such as short response time, safe remote operation, and high spatial definition due to the continuous monitoring [28], can be used for high precision and selective detection of pH value [6,28,29], temperature [21], biomass [30], and metal ions [31]. Now studying the influence of UV exposure, pH value, and metal ions on the PL intensity of the R-CQDs. Firstly, the PL intensity does not vary under continuous UV irradiation for hours for R-CQDs in pure ethanol and in high concentration of NaCl solution, indicating the prominent optical stability of the R-CQDs. Then 5 mL R-CQDs solution was put into respectively 6 mL NaOH solution (0.1 mg/mL, pH = 11.17) and 6 mL HCl solution (0.0036 mg/mL, pH = 4.22). The corresponding PL spectra are shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c), respectively. In comparison to the PL intensity shown in Fig. 3(a), the R-CQDs in NaOH solution can lead to a stronger PL intensity and the PL peak is blue-shifted, while the R-CQDs in HCl solution show a weaker PL intensity and the PL peak is redshifted. This is in line with the previous experimental findings [32]. In an acidic environment, the PL emission peak redshifts, the emission intensity decreases with increasing carboxyl group [32]. The corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s for R-CQDs in different solutions are shown in Fig. 3(d–f), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(e), the peak of hydroxyl (CO) is very strong and the peak of carboxyl (OCO) is weak for R-CQDs in NaOH solution, indicating that the amount of CO is much higher than that of OCO around the sp 2 carbon area. Also, Fig. 3(f) indicates that the amount of CO is less than that of OCO around the sp 2 carbon area for R-CQDs in HCl solution. The intensity of the PL emission increases with increasing the peak height of CO (COH) and with decreasing the peak height of OCO (see Fig. 3(a–c)). Thus, it can speculate that the PL intensity of the R-CQDs is related closely to the concentration of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Furthermore, Fig. 5 (a) and (b) shows the PL spectra of R-CQDs in solutions with different pH values at a fixed excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The relations between the PL intensities and the pH values are also shown in the insets where the gradual increase in Fig. 5(a) and decrease in Fig. 5(b) in the pH values are presented. In the range of pH values from 4 to 12, there is a good linear dependence between the pH value and the PL intensity as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(a). Interestingly, the dependence of the PL intensity on the pH values is reversible as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(b). For example, the PL intensity increases with pH values from 4 to 12 by adding NaOH, while it can decrease back to the initial position as the pH values are reduced from 12 to 4 by dropping HCl. This process can be repeated several times. Based on these observations, the PL intensity relates closely to the protonation and deprotonation of the R-CQDs can be concluded [30]. As shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d), according to the reaction equation: RCOOH + NaOH → RCOONa + H2O (where R stands for compounds linked to the carboxyl group), the part of the −COOH at the edge of the R-CQDs converts to −COONa by adding NaOH and reduces the amount of −COOH at the edge of sp 2-conjugated π-domains [32]. When HCl is added, according to the reaction equation: RCOONa + HCl→ RCOOH + NaCl, −COONa is returned to −COOH. Thus, the deprotonation degree of the R-CQDs increases with the pH values from 4 to 12, producing a higher concentration of hydroxy groups at the edges of the R-CQDs (see Fig. 5(d)). These hydroxy groups can serve as fluorophore species and, consequently, enhance the intensity of the PL emission. As the concentration of hydroxy groups approaches a maximum value, the dangling bonds on the surface of R-CQDs are nearly saturated, and the increasing rate of the PL intensity slows down (pH from 11 to 12). This optically pH-sensitive feature may render the R-CQDs to be attractive fluorescent probes for pH-monitoring in biological and biomedical applications.

Fig. 6 shows the TGA (blue curve) and DGT (red curve) curves of the R-CQDs. The mass loss occurs over three stages: below 150 °C, 150–350 °C, and 350–500 °C. In the first stage, a thermal decomposition peak appears at 122 °C in the DGT curve and a mass loss of up to 9.05 % can be measured. This amount of mass loss is attributed to the evaporation and dehydration of adsorbed water or solvent in R-CQDs [33]. In the second phase, the thermal decomposition peak appears at a temperature of 175 °C with a percentage of mass loss of about 22.75 %. This mass loss is attributed to the dehydration of interlayer water in R-CQDs and the thermal decomposition of the carboxyl group [34], which decomposes into CO2 at about 200 °C. This indicates that the thermal decomposition of R-CQDs mainly occurs at temperatures of 150–350 °C and that the greatest mass loss is approximately 31.8 %. The 10.57 % weight loss at the third stage of the TGA curve is attributed to the thermolysis of hydroxyl groups [35] on the surface of R-CQDs at about 416 °C. These findings indicate that the thermal decomposition behaviors of the R-CQDs are related to the thermal stability of dispersant and surface functional groups. Because the R-CQDs contains only 1.8 % of N, the effect of amino groups on the thermal behavior of R-CQDs is very weak.

It is known that the CQDs can be applied for selective and rapid detection of heavy metal ions [31]. Very recently, the fluorescent CQDs, synthesized from tobacco wastewater have been applied for the detection of ferric (III) ions [23]. In this study, the influences of the metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag2+, and Mg2+) are examined on the intensity of the PL emission from R-CQDs in a pure ethanol solution, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). As can be seen, the PL intensity or the amplitude of the PL peak from R-CQDs is irresponsive to most of the metal ions measured here, except it quenches largely after adding Cr3+ ions in the solution. Because only the presence of Cr3+ ions can lead to the quenching of the PL emission from CQDs, now the effect of Cr3+ on CQDS in ethanol solution is mainly discussed. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of the R-CQDs in pure ethanol solution and ethanol solution with Cr3+ ions are shown in Fig. 7(b). The absorption peak at 287 and 371 nm vanishes when adding Cr3+ ions into the R-CQDs solution, suggesting that Cr3+ ions have coordinated with oxygen- and nitrogen-rich groups in the surface and/or edges of the CQDs. Fig. 7(c) and (d) shows the PL spectra from R-CQDs in solutions with different concentrations of Cr3+ ions. As can be seen, under the same measurement conditions, the intensity (or peak amplitude) of PL emission from R-CQDs decreases gradually with increasing Cr3+ concentration N from 0 to 80 μM at a fixed excitation wavelength of 420 nm. In the regimes N=[0, 0.6] μM and N=[1, 80] μM, see insets in Fig. 7(c) and (d), the PL intensity (or peak amplitude) decreases almost linearly with increasing Cr3+ concentration via:
(2)
I =
1433.21 - 1510.71
N ;
N =
0 ,
0.6
μM,
931.39 - 10.56
N ;
N =
1 ,
80
μM
.

These results suggest that the R-CQDs can be applied for selective and rapid detection of Cr3+ ions in ethanol solutions, based on the effect that the PL emission from R-CQDs can be efficiently quenched by the presence of the acceptors such as Cr3+ [36]. Namely, the photo-excited R-CQDs can be good donors in the language of semiconductor physics.

Furthermore, Cr is No. 24 element and a Cr3 + ion has 21 electrons outside the core. The distribution of extra-nuclear electrons in Cr3 + is as follows: 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d3 . In general, metal ions such as Cr3+ can accept the electrons of ligands in the outer layer, and the electron pairs of hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups of CQDs can be used to fill the hybrid empty orbits of metal ions. Fig. 8 (a–c) respectively shows the coordination diagram of Cr3+ with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino functional groups on the surface/edge of R-CQDs. Compared with other groups with a negative charge, hydroxyl has a stronger activity to couple with Cr3+ because the electron pair in hydroxyl [37] is easier to fill in the d orbital of Cr3+ ion, shown in Fig. 8(a). Furthermore, the molecular orbitals of carbonyl are as follows: 6σ, 2π, 5σ, 1π, 4σ, and 3σ. Since most of the electronic cloud of orbit 4σ is located between the nuclei of carbonyl, the electron pair of 4σ cannot provide electrons to Cr3+. The electron of 3σ belongs to the arc pair electron of oxygen, and the electronegativity of oxygen is larger than that of carbon atom. It is difficult for oxygen to take out the electron pair of 3σ and give it to the central metal atom. Therefore, the molecular orbital which can easily form σ coordination with the central metal atom is 5σ (Fig. 8(b)). Also, there are also arc electron pairs on the amino group linked with the benzene ring, which coordinates with Cr3+ ions to form a five-membered ring (Fig. 8(c)). This is similar to the coordination of metal ions with amino groups in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) [38]. The schematic diagram of R-CQDS quenching process is shown in Fig. 8(d). As can be seen, in the presence of optical pumping, the electrons in the R-CQDs can be excited from lower occupied electronic states to higher empty states. The photo-excited electrons in higher energy states can be relaxed into the electronic states formed by the surface functional groups via non-radiative electronic transition channels [32]. In such a case, the arc pair electron is transferred into the empty d-orbitals of Cr3+ ions via non-radiative electronic transition channels. This is the main process responsible for the quenching of the PL emission from R-CQDs by adding Cr3+.

The detection limit and pH-sensitivity of different CQDs are listed in Table 1 . Compared with other published works [39–43], the obtained R-CQDs from this work exhibit a relatively higher detection limit and pH-sensitivity in the same measurement conditions. It indicates that the R-CQDs can be used for low-cost and sensitive probe of the pH values and of the metal ions in solutions.

In this work, the red-emitting CQDs (R-CQDs) were synthesized from wine lees by using a highly efficient, low-cost, and environment-friendly synthetic method. These R-CQDs dispersed in ethanol solution can exhibit strong PL emission with sharp red color. It has been found that the features of the PL spectra from R-CQDs are sensitive to the pH values in solutions from 4 to 12 and the pH-response of the PL intensity can be reversible. Furthermore, the results of this study demonstrate that the R-CQDs can be applied for selective and rapid detection of Cr3+ ions in solutions with Cr3+ ion concentrations from 0.1 μM to 80 μM through the simple and standard optical experiment, such as the PL measurement. There is no doubt that the interesting and important experimental findings from this work can be the basis for applying the CQDs as materials and devices for low-cost and sensitive probe of the pH values and the metal ions in solutions.
